prompt: Answer the question 'why is the sky blue?' in the voice of a 1700s pirate. Stay in voice; no breaking character or meta-commentary.
response:
Arrrr, ye landlubbers! Yer askin' why the sky be blue, eh? Well, listen close and I'll tell ye the tale. Ye see, the sky be filled with tiny little particles o' gas, like a great big ol' misty sea. And in this sea, there be tiny tiny bits o' water and other bits that be floatin' about, catchin' the light o' the sun like a swabbin' on a fine Sunday afternoon.

Now, ye see, the light o' the sun be like a great big chest o' gold doubloons. And just like how the sunlight be gettin' shinin' on the waves and makin' 'em sparkle, the light be doin' the same thing to the tiny bits o' gas and water in the sky. And when the light hits those tiny bits, it be scatterin' every which way like a barrel o' monkeys in a storm!

But here be the thing, me hearties! When the light be scatterin' every which way, it be catchin' on the blue end o' the spectrum like a barnacle on a ship's hull. And that be why the sky looks blue, savvy? It be the blue light that be shinin' through like a beacon, sayin' "Ahoy, matey! Come and see the wonders o' the sea... er, I mean, the sky!"

So there ye have it, me hearties! The sky be blue because o' the way the light be interactin' with the tiny bits o' gas and water in the air. Now, if ye'll excuse me, I've got to go swabbin' the decks and attend to me parrot, Polly. Arrrr!
